content: The same can be said for The Beatles. Just because you don't understand how this generation works, doesn't mean that yours is somehow better. There's pluses and minuses to every comparison and Madonna was definitely considered "weird" and almost always pushing the envelope with sexual connotations.

@Nina-vn7vh  It was two million dollars and the money went to the director, set designer, cinematographer, lighting, lighting technicians, cameras, film, carpenters, materials to build the set, set dressers, wardrobe stylists, make-up artists, clothing, casting directors, choreographers, dancers, soundstage rental,  video editors, hundreds of assistants had to be paid, plus the cost of catering. It took almost a week to film this video - the costs add up. Lets not forget there was also a cat to be paid and the cat wrangler, too, LOL. The video was made to imitate a movie and for 1989, this was a superior video in comparison to what else was on MTV most of the time. Notice there are actual sets and not some cheap GCI, the clothes are designer, the editing is above board and Express Yourself is often considered to be a masterpiece of that era. It is still considered a classic and its quite popular not only with Madonna fans but those who want to work in the film industry and art.

Okay, I’m just gonna say it. I get that everyone’s glazing it like a Krispy Kreme, but this movie was mid. It’s a few great scenes strung together by a whole lot of often less than hilarious dialogue, servicing a plot that’s thinner than tissue paper and relies so much on this ridiculous Marvel multiverse that they have a citation disguised as a joke about it in the script. It’s a Deadpool movie; I don’t want to have to know everything about the MCU to watch it. It gets so intrusive that they have to use a lot more expository dialogue to explain setpieces than any movie should really have to, which takes away from the quality of the whole thing. 
The characters don’t feel right either. The whole subplot with Inara (I don’t remember the character’s name 😂) leaving Wade doesn’t even begin to impact the movie, because she has five whole minutes of screen time. They’d already done the lost love story twice; doing it worse for a third time didn’t help. Wade giving up the swords didn’t make sense either, given who he is. The whole sad sack plot point lasts for all of ten minutes anyway, so what was the point of having it? We don’t see Dopinder, Negasonic, Colossus, or even Yukiyo at really any point, in favor of a cast of cameos who never last for more than a few minutes at a stretch.
The worst part is, most of the dialogue wasn’t even that funny. In the first one, I laughed every few minutes. In the second, it was a bit less, but still funny. This one had maybe three jokes I actually remember. They tried to turn a comedy into a drama without losing the comedic aspects, a la The Princess Bride, and fumbled the bag in continent-shaking fashion. This movie had no focus; it tried to do too much at once, and it shows.

In my opinion Madonna is the greatest video artist of all-time. I know people will have different opinions. But for me Madonna has had so many groundbreaking videos that are actual art.

While I agree, most don't understand what it must be like for our Madge. Can y'all imagine having been in the game, being a top pop, visual artist this long, highly-scrutinised, betrayed by radio, criticized for doing what we all must -- age? While many would grow old gracefully (whatever that means to them), how can Madonna be expected to easily give up all she's acquired and relegate herself to what others wish? I want Madonna to wholly screw wishing, hoping to be played by radio and focus on the art of the music. "Madame X" mostly did that, but still had some radio jams in the mix. One day, hopefully her next effort, will be wholly art-concentrated, but I understand her lingering need to still be young, cool and on top. It must be extremely difficult being Madonna, the Queen of Pop who isn't yet mentally prepared to turn the page.

@menchlucena1067  Madonna's influence reigns supreme. 
Her impact on pop culture fashion will never be topped, and Madonna changed the role of women in pop music.
She laid out the template for the modern female pop icon, Madonna was the 1st female pop star to project an image of control, drive and fierce independence.
She has been delivering striking fashion moments for more than 3 decades: lace wedding dress and Boy Toy belt, the iconic cone bra, corsets, ponytail, fishnet, leotard, leather jacket, lace bodysuit, high-heeled boots
Madonna defined the 1980's fashion - teal lace tights, strands of beads, rubber bracelets, religious jewelry and headscarf.

Madonna was the 1st to make collaborations between pop artists and fashion designers.
Jean Paul Gaultier (Blond Ambition Tour) and Dolce & Gabbana (The Girlie Show Tour) became power houses after working with Madonna.
In 1995, Madonna made appearing in fashion campaigns the norm when she starred in a campaign for Versace.
She made several successful Versace campaigns, being one of them, the inspiration to Dua Lipa's Future Nostalgia album cover.
Madonna worked with fashion photographers as Steven Meisel, Herb Ritts, Steven Klein

Madonna has time and again proven skeptics and critics wrong.  Early in her career she wrote, co-wrote or performed (songs written for her) mostly bouncy pop-dance beat songs...Holiday, Dress You Up, Into the Groove, Like A Virgin, Material Girl, etc. Until she recorded her first ballad—Crazy For You for a movie.  In her very first concert tour in 1985–the Virgin Tour (at Detroit on video) her live performance of Crazy For You was passionate, emotionally powerful and evocative—even the song’s original writers (who were skeptical that she can do ballads) were blown away.
In 1994 Madonna released Take A Bow—a ballad she co-wrote with Babyface.  It won the MTV Music Award for Best Female Video—where Madonna wore 1950’s era fashion and hairdo.  She sent a copy of this video and a four-page handwritten letter to Evita director Alan Parker where she lobbied (and convinced) Parker that she can handle the musical and look alike role in Evita.
Madonna in an interview said that she always felt she can be a very good film actress but (not until Evita came) she’s only had bit roles in mediocre films.  She wanted to prove that she cannot only play a leading role well but also perform Andrew Lloyd Webber/Tim Rice ballads in Evita as well as any singer at that time—Her Golden Globe awards for her role and the Oscar for the ballad—You Must Love Me—vindicated her artistry and dedication to her craft.

I really appreciate the costume choice here wearing Victorian-style ballroom dress. It's not only aesthetically pleasing for a fantastic performance, but it has soooooo much cultural history tied to voguing. I was born in 1991 so I missed all the fanfare and discussions being had surrounding this, but I doubt there was much credit given to the persons who initiated ballroom culture and voguing, and that is the queer community largely made up of Blacks and Latinos. My hope is that now, as 30 years later we still appreciate this wonderful work of art, an effort is made to learn and appreciate the culture that inspired Madonna in the first place - the underground POC queer community.

Live To Tell was the dawning of the Philosopher Madonna. While Frozen is when Madonna becomes a Prophet.

content: yep

Thats only possible if if was originally filmed in analog on physical film if it was then it can be scanned into 4k (as is the case with Wham's Last Christmas https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E8gmARGvPlI) the quality that the original film holds is down to the camera operator. That being said most high budget music videos were shot on video film rather than tape.

The issue is that alot of media in the 80s onwards was filmed onto video tape rather than film. The data is stored digitally on the magnetic tape instead of individual frames as captured by film so can't be scanned. 

Another issue can be editing for example star trek the next generation was filmed on video film but then converted to tape to add the special effects so if they wanted to release a 4k version they would need to redo all the special effects of the show. 

It is possible to use Ai programs to enhance digital files ect which can result in large quaility improvements but where possible scanning original film is better as scanning a hd copy from the original film rather than trying to digitially improve a low quaility image.

But lastly It would be great to see all of Maddona's videography rescanned for the hd era. Her works are some of the most iconic short films/music videos of all time.

content:
@endokuken1678  Lady Gaga was very different. She start working with other artists and presenting some early songs on clubs from 2005-2007 collaborating on other artists records. By 2008 she released her first single and album that was her best seller album until today (Madonna first album did very well but it's not her best selling). She did have her 2 first singles to be #01 (and her best sellings ever worldwide) but she didn't kept climbing. After 4 or 5 years after releasing her first Album Lady Gaga didn't have the 2 best selling albums worldwide by a female artist (in fact her 3rd was not a huge seller), the most #01's in US and UK or was beating Guiness Records for biggest audience for a paid concert like Madonna did (130k for her Paris 1987 Who's That Girl Tour - and Madonna still holds this record until TODAY 33 years later!).


  . Two of her singles even missed the TOP 100 during this time and from the other 6 TOP 100 songs only 3 were TOP 40 (and one of them TOP 20). 

   She also had 3 more TOP 40 (2 of them TOP 20 Hits and 2 songs that missed the TOP 40 (but were close peaking #42 and #46). Madonna missed the TOP 100 for the first time 21 years into her carreer when "Hollywood" missed the HOT 100 due to radio backlash related to Madonna anti-war attitude and banned video (the song was still able to peak #04 on Billboard Single Sales Chart despite not having airplay as MTV still played the video).


had 4 #01's (including first 2 songs - very impressive) and 10 TOP 10 Hits. But in bettween Lady Gaga had another 11 songs that missed the TOP 10 (7 even of them missed the TOP 40 showing her charting inconsistency). On the following 7 years Lady Gaga would score another 3 TOP 10 Hits (including 2 aditional #01's) and another 8 TOP 100 Hits (including  7 TOP 40 Hits where 2 of them peaked TOP 20). 

 missed TOP 10 with 2 singles from her first album and then had 19 TOP 10's  (where 18 were consecutive) including 5 #01's and 4 #02's songs (including a song to #01 by Madonna herself). On the following 7 years Madonna scored another 19 TOP 10 singles ( and from 84 to 94 got an all time 34 consecutive Top 10 Hits) including 2 more #01's and 3 more #02's. By that time Madonna already had 37 TOP 10 hits in UK.
 
Looking at Madonna UK Charts history she got 63 TOP 10 Hits (is 3rd of all time, Michael Jackson follows with just 44)  and another all time record 68 consecutive TOP 20 songs on the charts (all her releases from 1984 until 2008 when "Miles Away" peaked #37 were TOP 20 Hits - almost all TOP 10's also). I talked about her #01's and #02's as besides today she having 13 #01 songs and being the female artist with more #01's she also scored a total of 12 #02's being the artist (among all) with most #02's hits.

2020, and still my breath is taken away by this 1992 masterpiece. It was rumoured that Madonna was at "zero percent body fat" when she shot this...with none other than the genius Mark Romanek (ILYSM). Entirely filmed in black and white, each frame is handpainted, hence the vivid blue that comes off as warm and cool at the same time. Ryuichi Sakamoto plays the director, and the minimalist Zen Japanese mood contrasts ever so beautifully with the industrial studio setting. Also, the electronica is simmering in this, boding so ever her revolution when "Ray of Light" came in. Masterclass, masterpiece. One of Madonna's finest!!! <3 <3 <3 <3 <3

In March during her day off from the filming, Madonna completed a remixed version (in an official video) of Love Don’t Live Here Anymore which was filmed in an abandoned hotel in Buenos Aires. Her rendition was evocative, passionate and the vocal training she took for Evita showed in the deeper tone and higher range in this song which she first covered as the only non-original number in her 1984 Like A Virgin album.  This ballad 
was originally recorded in 1978 by the soul, R&B group Rose Royce (lead vocals by Gwen Dickey)—it was one of Madonna’s pre-stardom personal favorites.  She also included the song in her 1995 album, Something to Remember , a compilation of 14 ballads she had recorded over that last decade prior to completing Evita...just to remind music critics and all that she has and can do ballads not just pop-beat dance music.

A true story. I was doing work experience in a local radio station here in the West of Ireland in 1990. Vogue had just come out and I had to play it so I went into the empty 'Off Air' studio to listen to it. I put on the 45 single and knocked up the volume dancing away to it. What I didn't know was that I had accidentally gone live on air knocking off the other studio and giving listeners the first air play of Vogue. Needless to say there was pandemonium with the station controller going mad with me over my 'minor' indiscretion. So that's how the radio listeners in the West of Ireland heard Vogue for the first time.

Gaga just fought “more” because it was more normalized at her time. In Madonna’s time, she couldn’t do that much because things were different back then. She did everything she did WITHOUT INTERNET and on a very homophobic and patriarchal world. She is the one who allowed artists like Gaga to exist. Gaga did not “decided to withdraw”, she just flopped a lotmwith Artpop, that’s why she stepped back from the industry highlights after 2013.

- Madonna has some of the best music videos on the industry, so that’s completely subjective, not a fact. 
- Madonna has great songs as Gaga does, so it’s also subjective, but Madonna has an advantage at that point ‘cause she has a lot more hits than Gaga,  as much as #1’s and top10s.

- In the same way you said the bullshit abou Marilyn Monroe, I also can say ALL Gaga’s work is by coping Madonna (what she really was accused for a very long time for doing), even more if you analyse things as Born This Way being clearly a copy/influenced by Express Yourself, or for Gaga using the same kind of controversy and polemics to base her career which Madonna also used. 
I give Gaga the better voice and better behavior, she really does have these things.

In addition, lots of people in the time were really uncomfortable with interracial relationships of any kind because they were racist, and seeing a "white man's daughter" being remotely affectionate with a black man got those racists all hot and bothered.  Dancing in front of a field of burning crosses is offensive to just about everybody...either because finding a burning cross in your yard means you are being targeted by the Klan or a more generalized objection to having the symbol of your faith set on fire if that doesn't bother you.  Madonna having an oopsie with a knife that resulted in stigmata (i.e. the wounds of Jesus Christ on the cross) set off the Catholics about as badly as telling them that their Church willingly assists pedophiles in evading justice.  Finally, nothing sets off White Christians like Black Jesus.

So that's the list of reasons people may find the video controversial.  I myself don't have any of the aforementioned hang-ups, but I was a small child in 1989 and remember everybody where I live (in Alabama) thinking it was a big deal.

content: Same! Lawd!😂

Listen to this record, ironically also produced by Shep Pettibone, and you can hear clearly the stolen horns and pretty much the entire baseline that they took from it. Salsoul Records sued Madonna & Shep. The lawsuit lasted for 25 years, if you can believe that. And the record company suing them lost. That was so unfair. Because clearly listening to the original disco record, you can hear what they took. They even took the man’s voice in the background saying love break love break love break. I don’t know who paid off who, but somebody settled that lawsuit unfairly. They never received a dime for the sampling. Salsoul Records deserved royalties from Vogue. And the story behind “Vogue” is really interesting. Madonnas record label gave her and Shep Pettibone a meager budget of only $5000 to produce the song. Madonna recorded her vocals in a very small phone booth size room in a shotty recording studio in lower Manhattan in 1989. She came up with the rap of the movie stars on her own. But Shep was the mastermind behind the entire sound. He had produced a remix of “Ooh, I Love It” by The Salsoul Orchestra in 1980. So, he took from what he had done and created Vogue. He just made the baseline. Take care and heavier. Speed it up a little. Gave it a harder kick drum. And a little piano chord eight keys. How they lost the lawsuit, is beyond my understanding. Because you can clearly hear the sections they took this song from the disco record. They also sampled MFSB, “love is the message”. Listen to both of those songs and tell me that you do not hear Vogue in them. https://youtu.be/HkDmbJegNpw

content: I see it differently. I see it as Madonna (who I loved) being a fading star in 1990 as house music made by anonymous people in Belgium and Italy was crashing into the European pop charts. So Madonna got her producers to get a TR-909 and a piano sound from Italo house to make herself credible again. This song was clearly inspired by club tracks like Black Box's 'Ride on Time', the biggest-selling record in the UK the year before. In addition, voguing was also old hat by 1990. Malcolm McLaren did a vogue record (actually produced and mixed by Mark Moore and William Orbit) based on the 'Paris is Burning' film in 1989. In short, this was Madonna's attempt at making a house record of the sort that had existed in underground clubs for a few years.

content:
Fun fact: In the film "The Cannonball Run (1981)", Roger Moore plays a character, a rich British playboy who believes himself to be Roger Moore and a James Bond wannabe. The characters spoof both himself and James Bond. Also, Jackie Chan made one of his earliest Western cameo in this action/comedy film, which was inspired by a real cross-country outlaw illegal racing, mixed with perhaps the Wacky Races cartoon. 

Speaking of James Bond, Cannonball Run and err..Jackie Chan. They also made a bunch of very similar parody films in Hong Kong. Sam Hui starred as the main actor in the "Aces Go Places" series started in 1982. It's an action/comedy series similar to veins above and also parodies James Bond. 

In "Aces Go Places 3", it's chock full of references and "unofficial/unlicensed" cameos of the characters from Bond films. They actually had a "Connery" Bond cameo in it, very much like the Cannonball Run's Roger Moore, but they actually had Sean Connery's young brother, Neil Connery to play the faux/clone Bond instead!

content:
@shanehughes3511  In the defence of Gen Z, they don't get exposed to Madonna, because of her age. In the UK, for example, radio stations that cater for younger listeners don't play songs by artists they deem to be too old, and less and less young people listen to the radio anyway. Streaming, as wonderfully accessible as it is, is taking away the ability that radio always had to make someone aware of an artist even if they didn't like them. Recommendations get made on streaming platforms, but someone like Madonna - with her constant ability to make bang-on-trend music (when she's not already setting a trend, that is) - will not get the same exposure she used to because she's not young enough anymore.
Also, it's only natural for young people to initially stick to current music: it's what we all do. But they'll come to discover her in time, when they, too, are a bit older.

A humble suggestion to Madonna:
Album name: ‘Zephyr in the Sky’
An appeal by the fans to release a compilation of 20 masterpieces from Madonna’s catalogue personifying every emotion we have been through in this most dark age. And as we steer back to humanity and life. Search for the future.
A journey to the light, there is always a ray of hope and then the brilliant radiance of life.
Emotions and these songs clutch in perpetuity, the journey we continue.
 
 
Ray of Light
The deliverance of hope. The rhythm of life never seizes. There will always be that zephyr in the sky each night. Embrace it we can.
 
Masterpiece
Preservation of our most precious in peril. Our children, our people. Our kind. Caress them, protect them, enliven them.
 
Crazy
Souls without direction. Souls succumbing to the anarchy. Moments of lapse and dissolution of reason. There is always a method in any madness.
 
Miles Away
Separation - a reality, distancing in the new remote ecosystem – a way of life. Now from a far. Life anew.
 
4 Minutes
There were heroes. And there were those who saved the world, those who ultimately will and those who arrogantly don’t. We can all be heroes, for even more than a day.
 
Borderline
Barriers, walls, borders can neither prevent the triumphant human, nor conquer the invisible enemy. Humanity on the borderline of distraught. Never destroyed.
 
Die Another Day
Death can never be normalized. Not one, not a million. Fight. Death will come, in victory there will ultimately be death. But another day, its not our time to go.
 
You’ll See
Strength is personal, strength is contagion, strength prevails, strength is now our constant. You’ll see.
 
La Isla Bonita
My dreams are always my own and even I don’t control them. There will be sun again, there will be celebration again, we will hold hands again –into that beautiful sunset.
 
Ghost Town
The cradles of our civilization – devoid of voices, people, laughter, love. Ghostly in silence, our great cities shall live again – thrive they shall.
 
Like a Prayer
God. Life is a mystery, everyone must stand alone. You will hear the voice and I always hear you call my name. Forgive me. Pray.
 
Joan of Arc
You’re only human. Our weakness it can never be. A Joan in us all. New awakenings.
 
Sorry
Not so many need to be sorry. But sorry are those who remain bigoted in normalizing death and cheating life. They must be sorry. Usher in the preservers. Change.
 
Another Suitcase in Another Hall
In the anarchy of this world, don’t lose yourself. ‘I’ never goes out of fashion. You are not another brick in the wall.
 
Dress You Up
Making love, satiated the nights of darkness, all over, all over my body, mind and soul. The adrenaline of sex, of lust, of expressing it still.
 
Vogue
At night we lock the door when no one else can see. There was a time with my people around me. I’m good with me for now. I n deed to dance, you can too.
 
Human Nature
Express yourself don’t repress yourself. Humans we are humane but he not always is. The Emperors of yore and now shall not be remembered – not those who lacked humanity and mocked death.
 
I rise
I am talking about a revolution. A revolution of love. Of compassion. Of kindness. Our time is now and we shall not let the autumn leaves fall in vain. All rise and realize your own renaissance – for you and for the world.
 
I’ll Remember
I’ll remember the love. I’ll remember the love you gave me. Now that we’re standing on our own – we shall walk again together, we shall celebrate again together. Another circus, another show.
Music
Its about the music. Its always been about the music. Music helped me breathe this, live this, survive this. Music makes the people come together – yeah.
 
In gratitude, Madonna.

content:
Madonna’s 1989 music video for "Like a Prayer" is a dense, symbolic work that blends religious ecstasy with sexual desire and social justice. Directed by Mary Lambert, it was intentionally provocative, aiming to challenge racial stereotypes and the traditional boundaries of the Catholic Church. Core Narrative and Social Meaning. The video follows a specific storyline that acts as a social commentary on racism and injustice: 
* The Injustice: Madonna plays a witness to a murder committed by white supremacists. An innocent Black man (played by actor Leon Robinson) is arrested for the crime by police who act on racial bias.
* The Sanctuary: Seeking safety and moral strength, Madonna flees to a church. She sees a statue of a saint that resembles the arrested man.
* The Transformation: The saint (often identified as Saint Martin de Porres, the patron saint of mixed-race people) comes to life in a dream sequence. He kisses her, symbolizing a spiritual and emotional union.
* The Resolution: Empowered by her spiritual experience, Madonna goes to the police station to testify, clearing the innocent man's name. 
Key Symbols and Themes
* Religious vs. Sexual Ecstasy: Madonna and director Mary Lambert both stated the song explores the "mystery and magic" of Catholicism and the link between sexual and religious passion. Lyrics like "I'm down on my knees, I wanna take you there" are widely interpreted as having a dual meaning.
* Burning Crosses: While typically a symbol of the Ku Klux Klan and racial terror, Madonna used them to represent the "political and social extremities of racism" and to show herself dancing defiantly against that hate.
* Stigmata: Madonna is shown with bleeding palms (stigmata), a Catholic sign of holiness and suffering, symbolizing her personal transformation and internal struggle with Catholic guilt.
* "The End" Curtain: The video ends with a curtain falling, revealing the story was a stage play. This suggests that the audience is part of the "performance" of social justice and morality. Impact and Controversy
The video was so controversial that the Vatican condemned it as blasphemous. This backlash led to Pepsi to cancel a $5 million sponsorship deal with Madonna, though she was famously allowed to keep the money.

content:
For me Madonna will always be beautiful. What she did for the lgbtq community, how she empowered women,, she changed the whole landscape within pop culture and she has always raised her voice for those who couldn’t. 
And not to mention the epic music videos. “Like a prayer” was a huge controversy when it came out. Due to the fact that she portrayed Jesus as a black man (though he was definitely not Caucasian) and had burning crosses as a metaphor for all the pain the Catholic Church (and all religion) has caused.
And the music… Best selling artist of all time.

For me personally there is a beauty in that that can’t be ignored. 
She was never the “pretty girl” and I believe a lot of people was very inspired seeing a spunky, opinionated and talented girl breaking through and having success.

So to boil it down to. Don’t point fingers at Madonna for having cosmetic work done. She is also a human being, a woman growing older in show business.
Point fingers at the norms and the beauty standards.

Remember, she did this in an age where much of her artistry was considered taboo.  Artists today have no shock quality like she had.
